intro.narrative:
It's late October in central New England. You drove alone to a trailhead for what was supposed to be a 4-hour out-and-back. You told no one where you were going.

It's now 4:30 PM. You've been hiking since 10 AM and took a wrong fork somewhere. You don't recognize anything around you. The temperature was 52°F when you started — it's dropping fast and the sky is going gray. Sunset is around 5:15 PM.
gear: Half-eaten granola bar and an apple
intro.threat_summary: Cold exposure, darkness, no one knows your location

decision_points.situation: You realize you're lost. It's 4:30 PM, you have less than an hour of daylight, and you're not sure which direction leads back to the trailhead. What's your first move?

options.text: Keep moving — try to find a trail, road, or high ground before dark
options.consequence: Moving without a plan in unfamiliar terrain burns energy and daylight. You could deepen your lostness.
options.teaching_point: Random movement is one of the most dangerous things a lost person can do. Stop, breathe, assess.

options.text: Stop now, find a defensible spot, and start preparing for an overnight
options.consequence: You preserve daylight for shelter building and make yourself easier to find by staying put. This is the survival-trained response.
options.teaching_point: The rule is STOP: Stop, Think, Observe, Plan. Staying put is almost always the right call when lost.

options.text: Try to climb the nearest high point to get your bearings and maybe cell signal
options.consequence: It takes 25 minutes of hard scrambling to reach the top. You're now sweaty, exposed, and it's nearly dark. No signal.
options.teaching_point: High ground for signal is a reasonable instinct, but it burns irreplaceable daylight and leaves you exposed at dusk.

options.text: Follow the nearest stream or drainage downhill
options.consequence: Streams do often lead to civilization, but in dense New England forest this can take hours and lead through brutal terrain in the dark.
options.teaching_point: Following water out is a valid long-term strategy, but not the right first move when you still have daylight to prep shelter.

decision_points.situation: You're exposed on the high point, damp from exertion, wind cutting through your fleece. It's 4:55 PM. You spotted a stream drainage in the valley below and a dense stand of conifers about 150 feet downslope. You have maybe 20 minutes of usable daylight. What do you prioritize?

options.text: Descend fast toward that stream drainage — water is your most important resource
options.consequence: You reach the stream in the dark. Your water bottle wasn't the crisis. You've burned your daylight on a resource you didn't urgently need.
options.teaching_point: In cold weather survival, priority order is: warmth, shelter, water, food. Most people invert this because thirst feels urgent and cold feels manageable — until it isn't.

options.text: Get down into those conifers and start building shelter before it's fully dark
options.consequence: You reach the hemlocks with 15 minutes of gray light to spare and start working immediately. You're cold but in control.
options.teaching_point: Shelter before water, always, in cold exposure scenarios. The conifers block wind and provide building material.

options.text: Use your lighter to start a signal fire up here on the rocks while you still have visibility
options.consequence: You're above treeline with no fuel. The lighter produces nothing useful and you've wasted precious minutes.
options.teaching_point: Signal fires require fuel. Rock outcrops have none. Never attempt a fire without confirming you have the materials to sustain it.

options.text: Stay put, conserve energy, and use your phone flashlight to signal through the night
options.consequence: You survive the night exposed with no shelter, burning battery on a flashlight nobody is looking for yet. Hypothermia risk is high.
options.teaching_point: Phone light signaling only works if someone is actively searching. On night one, before anyone knows you're missing, it's just battery drain.

decision_points.situation: You're under the hemlocks in the dark. Hands getting clumsy — early sign of heat loss. Temp is probably high 30s. You have your lighter. What do you do first?

options.text: Gather wood and get a fire going immediately — warmth is critical
options.consequence: Fire helps, but without shelter you're radiating heat into open air all night. Fire alone won't keep you warm in these conditions.
options.teaching_point: A fire with no shelter is inefficient. Shelter retains body heat even with no fire at all. Do both, but shelter first.

options.text: Build a debris shelter first, then worry about fire
options.consequence: You spend 40 minutes building a hemlock debris shelter. It's rough but wind-blocking. Your core temp stabilizes from the exertion. Then you build fire.
options.teaching_point: Ground insulation is the most critical element — the earth steals heat faster than cold air. Boughs underneath you first, always.

options.text: Drink some water, eat your granola bar, then build a fire
options.consequence: Calories help, but the time cost of eating first when your hands are already getting clumsy is dangerous. Heat loss is accelerating.
options.teaching_point: Food and water matter, but not when you're actively losing core temperature. Shelter and warmth always outrank nutrition in acute cold exposure.

options.text: Layer up with everything you have and build fire at the same time
options.consequence: Multitasking works, but clumsy hands make fire-starting harder and slower. You end up with a mediocre shelter and a mediocre fire.
options.teaching_point: Sequencing matters. Do one thing well rather than two things poorly when resources and time are scarce.

decision_points.situation: You need fire. You have a lighter but it's dark and you didn't pre-collect tinder while you could still see. How do you approach getting a fire started?

options.text: Dig into the duff and dead leaves at the base of the hemlocks — dry tinder is usually just below the surface
options.consequence: You find some dry material but it's inconsistent and takes many attempts to catch. The lighter gets warm from repeated use.
options.teaching_point: Duff can work but is unreliable. It often contains moisture and doesn't shred into the fine fibers that catch a spark reliably.

options.text: Strip the dry inner bark from a dead standing branch — it shreds into fine fibers
options.consequence: Good natural tinder. It takes a few minutes to prepare but catches well. Fire is going within 10 minutes.
options.teaching_point: Dead standing wood is always drier than ground wood. Inner bark of many species shreds into excellent natural tinder.

options.text: Check your pockets and pack for anything man-made that burns well as a starter
options.consequence: You find a granola bar wrapper, a receipt, and cardboard from a pack tag. Good accelerants but burn fast — you need natural tinder underneath.
options.teaching_point: Man-made materials are excellent fire starters but burn too quickly to sustain. They need natural tinder underneath to bridge to real fuel.

options.text: Both B and C — layer man-made tinder with natural fiber for the best chance of a catch
options.consequence: Cardboard base, bark fiber nest, wrapper in the center. First flick catches. Fire is sustainable within 15 minutes.
options.teaching_point: Layering tinder types — man-made accelerant over natural fiber over coarse material — maximizes catch probability and minimizes lighter use.

decision_points.situation: It's around 8 PM. You're stable — fire going, rough shelter built. Battery at 26%, still no signal. What's your overnight strategy?

options.text: Conserve battery completely — no phone use until morning when you'll move toward the stream and follow it out
options.consequence: Safe but passive. Nobody knows where you are. If weather turns or you're injured tomorrow, you have no communication attempt logged.
options.teaching_point: Battery conservation matters, but a text costs almost nothing and has enormous upside. Passive survival is a last resort, not a first choice.

options.text: Send your GPS coordinates as a text to someone — texts sometimes push through even without a full signal bar
options.consequence: The message queues, sits for 90 seconds, then shows a single checkmark. It went out. You enable airplane mode to conserve battery and set a 90-minute sleep alarm.
options.teaching_point: SMS uses a lower-bandwidth channel than voice or data and can push through on weak signal. It's a low-cost, high-upside move that most people don't know to try.

options.text: Set a 90-minute sleep alarm, tend the fire, sleep in cycles through the night
options.consequence: Smart fire management and rest cycling are solid survival technique. But you've missed the communication opportunity.
options.teaching_point: Sleep cycling to maintain a fire is legitimate survival strategy — 90 minutes aligns with a full sleep cycle. But pair it with a communication attempt first.

options.text: Keep the phone flashlight on low all night so rescuers might spot it from above
options.consequence: Phone dies by 2 AM. No one is searching yet — you're not officially missing until morning at the earliest. The fire is a far better signal.
options.teaching_point: Flashlight signaling only works when someone is looking. Your fire is visible for miles. Your phone light is visible for hundreds of feet. Use the right tool.

debrief.title: Scenario Debrief
debrief.core_lesson: In cold-weather survival, the priority order is warmth first, shelter second, water third, food last. Most people invert this because thirst feels urgent and cold feels manageable — until it isn't.

A.label: Expert Survivor
A.description: You think like someone trained for this. Your instincts are calibrated and your decisions were sequenced correctly.

B.label: Capable Survivor
B.description: Solid instincts with a few costly mistakes. You made it through but left margins on the table.

C.label: Struggling Survivor
C.description: You survived, but more by luck than design. Some fundamental priorities need recalibration.

D.label: At Risk
D.description: Critical knowledge gaps in cold-weather survival. Study the debrief carefully — these mistakes are life-threatening in real conditions.
